meta.title: Eisbach Wave Tracker
meta.headline: As of April 22, 2026, the Eisbach wave is still officially closed, but reconstruction work has clearly moved back into active testing.
meta.summary: The famous Munich surf wave did not reliably re-form after the fall 2025 channel cleanup removed sediment and altered the flow geometry. Since then, the issue has become part engineering problem, part safety debate, and part political fight. The current state is not a reopening: Munich still considers the existing whitewater too dangerous. But the city, the surf community, and engineering experts are now openly testing ways to rebuild a stable, legal, and safer wave.
meta.outlook: Expect more approved trial installs, then a decision on legal responsibility and a final rebuild concept before any full reopening.
meta.nextWatch: Watch for an official Munich statement confirming the newest reported ramp-test window and for any update on whether the city can meet the promised summer-holiday reopening target.

facts.label: Current legal status
facts.value: Surfing is banned until further notice.
facts.detail: Munich issued a fresh general order on February 11, 2026 because the spot had turned into dangerous whitewater.

facts.label: Physical wave state
facts.value: Usually unstable whitewater, not a dependable green wave.
facts.detail: The wave briefly resurfaced around Easter 2026, then faded again within days.

facts.label: Main blockers
facts.detail: The debate is no longer just about surfing technique; it is now also about legal responsibility and how to create a repeatable wave shape.

facts.label: Engineering lead
facts.detail: His tests with the surf community are the most concrete technical path back to a stable wave.

facts.label: Political target
facts.detail: Dominik Krause repeated that goal in the city's April 16, 2026 update.

facts.label: Why a permanent install matters
facts.value: Without one, the city says the wave appears only 30 to 40 days a year.
facts.detail: That strongly suggests a durable engineered solution is being treated as the realistic path.

watchlist.title: Confirmation of the latest test window
watchlist.detail: BILD reported on April 18 that approved ramp trials had started for four weeks. That is promising, but it still needs matching confirmation from a primary official source.

watchlist.title: Liability model for a rebuilt wave
watchlist.detail: The hardest unresolved issue is who carries legal responsibility if the wave is rebuilt as a managed, quasi-official sports site.

watchlist.title: Whether the summer deadline holds
watchlist.detail: If Munich misses the summer-holiday goal, the political argument around bureaucracy versus safety will intensify again.

coverage: Official Munich city statements and legal notices
Engineering and university reporting tied to the reconstruction experiments
IGSM and other public surf-community signals
Recent German news coverage, especially dpa-based reports
Publicly indexed social references when available; no private or login-walled scraping

feed.title: Newest report says approved ramp tests have started
feed.summary: BILD reported that selected surfers had begun day-by-day testing of different ramp constructions under an approved four-week test window, with results to be passed back to the city. That would mark the clearest move yet from argument into formal trial operation.
feed.impact: Positive movement, but still not a reopening

feed.title: Munich says the goal is still to restore surfing at the Eisbach
feed.summary: In its April 16, 2026 statement, the city said approved experiments with Prof. Robert Meier-Staude and surfers were ongoing, called the tests promising, and added that fallback design work was also being developed with university experts if the current trial path fails.
feed.impact: Strong confirmation that restoration is active

feed.title: Report points to a three-part hardwood ramp as the leading design
feed.summary: A t-online report, citing the Abendzeitung, said the conclusion from recent tests favored a three-part hardwood ramp fixed to the bridge pillar and concrete side walls. It is one of the strongest technical clues about what a rebuilt wave might actually look like.
feed.impact: Technical path is getting more concrete

feed.title: The Easter comeback vanishes almost immediately
feed.summary: After briefly returning around Karfreitag, the wave nearly disappeared again. Reports said a submerged grid may have helped it rise for a moment, showing how fragile and contested the current state still is.
feed.impact: Confirms the wave is still unstable

feed.title: Wave briefly reappears before Easter and reignites the debate
feed.summary: The standing wave unexpectedly returned for a short window, drawing surfers and onlookers back to the spot. At the same time, Dominik Krause renewed the political target of getting the wave back for the summer holidays.

feed.title: Around 250 people protest the surf ban
feed.summary: A public demonstration pushed back against the continuing closure and showed that the wave had become a civic and political issue, not just a sports story.

feed.title: IGSM turns the Eisbach into a local-election issue
feed.summary: The surf association published candidates' positions and framed the Eisbach as public cultural infrastructure. That made it clear the future of the wave now sits inside Munich's wider political identity debate.

feed.title: Munich formally bans surfing because the spot is too dangerous
feed.summary: The city said the partial wave that still formed had turned into mostly non-surfable whitewater and posed an immediate drowning risk, so it imposed a new general ban on surfing at the site.

feed.title: An Instagram video from the mayor signals cautious optimism
feed.summary: A dpa report said then-mayor Dieter Reiter posted an Instagram video from the first reconstruction attempt and said it did not look bad. It was one of the rare public social signals from city leadership during the repair push.

feed.title: City and surf groups agree on a project trial
feed.summary: Munich announced a project attempt to test how the wave could be restored, with the surf groups carrying responsibility during the trial and the city handling technical checks on the structures.

feed.title: First pilot test proves the wave can be pushed back toward a surfable shape
feed.summary: The university's on-site test with wooden kickers briefly created a roughly three-meter green wave before it collapsed again into whitewater. That did not solve the problem, but it showed the geometry could be influenced.

feed.title: After the fall cleanup, the wave fails to return
feed.summary: Public reporting in early November documented the core problem: after the annual cleanup and sediment removal, the Eisbach wave did not build back into its usual standing form.

sources.label: City of Munich, April 16, 2026
sources.note: Best primary source for the current legal status, active experiments, natural-wave limits, and Krause's summer-holiday target.

sources.label: Munich legal notice, February 11, 2026
sources.note: Primary source for the still-active surf ban and the city's description of the whitewater danger.

sources.label: City project-trial agreement, January 12, 2026
sources.note: Shows when the city and surf groups moved from deadlock toward a formal reconstruction trial.

sources.label: Hochschule München pilot report, November 27, 2025
sources.note: Most direct technical description of how the wave behaves under controlled intervention.

sources.label: IGSM February 2026 archive
sources.note: Useful for understanding how central the wave has become in Munich's civic and political identity.

sources.label: ZEIT / dpa Easter comeback coverage
sources.note: Best recent reporting on the wave's brief return and how quickly it collapsed again.

sources.label: t-online ramp-design report
sources.note: Important for the current leading design idea, but it relies on cited reporting rather than a fresh city publication.

sources.label: BILD on approved April tests
sources.note: Currently the freshest signal that approved trials may already be active, but it should still be confirmed against primary sources.
